report_name: Microsoft Dismantles "StegoAd," a Malicious Edge Extension Campaign Using Steganography

threat_actors.name: DarkSpectre
threat_actors.description: A Chinese threat actor linked to the StegoAd campaign, which used 119 malicious browser extensions to perform ad fraud and steal credentials via steganography.

attack_patterns.name: Drive-by Compromise
attack_patterns.description: Users were tricked into installing malicious extensions from the legitimate Microsoft Edge Add-ons store, which served as the primary initial access vector.

attack_patterns.name: Hidden Files and Directories
attack_patterns.description: The campaign used steganography to hide malicious JavaScript payloads by appending them to PNG files after the IEND marker, evading static analysis.

attack_patterns.name: Time Based Checks
attack_patterns.description: The malicious extensions remained dormant for several days post-installation to build user trust and avoid immediate suspicion before activating their payload.

attack_patterns.name: JavaScript
attack_patterns.description: Malicious JavaScript, concealed within image or font files, was decoded and executed within the context of the user's browser to carry out ad fraud and data theft.

attack_patterns.name: Keylogging
attack_patterns.description: The StegoAd malware was capable of capturing login credentials from specific login pages, including Google and WordPress.

attack_patterns.name: Steal Web Session Cookie
attack_patterns.description: The malware exfiltrated browser cookies, allowing the threat actor to perform session hijacking and impersonate users on various websites.

attack_patterns.name: Web Protocols
attack_patterns.description: The malicious extension established a command and control channel, likely using standard web protocols, to receive and execute arbitrary code from a remote server.

malware.name: StegoAd
malware.description: A malicious payload hidden in 119 different browser extensions using steganography. It performed ad fraud, credential theft from Google and WordPress, and exfiltrated browser cookies for session hijacking.

campaigns.name: StegoAd
campaigns.description: A large-scale, long-running malicious browser extension campaign on the Microsoft Edge Add-ons store that used steganography to hide payloads for ad fraud and credential theft. The campaign involved 119 extensions and affected up to 2.6 million users.

identities.name: Microsoft Edge Add-ons store
identities.description: The legitimate marketplace where the 119 malicious StegoAd extensions were hosted for over two years.

identities.name: Google
identities.description: Login credentials for Google accounts were targeted for theft by the StegoAd malware.

identities.name: WordPress
identities.description: Login credentials for WordPress sites were targeted for theft by the StegoAd malware.

identities.name: Amazon
identities.description: An e-commerce site whose affiliate links were hijacked by the StegoAd malware for ad fraud.

identities.name: eBay
identities.description: An e-commerce site whose affiliate links were hijacked by the StegoAd malware for ad fraud.

infrastructure.name: StegoAd C2 Server
infrastructure.description: A remote server used to send arbitrary code to the malicious browser extensions and receive exfiltrated credentials and cookies.

indicators.name: StegoAd-like Anomalous Outbound Connections from Browser
indicators.pattern:
title: StegoAd-like Anomalous Outbound Connections from Browser
id: f1a2b3c4-d5e6-4f7a-8b9c-0d1e2f3a4b5c
status: experimental
description: Detects potentially malicious browser extensions making unexpected outbound network connections to domains not associated with normal browsing activity, similar to the TTPs of the StegoAd campaign.
references:
  - https://thehackernews.com/2026/06/microsoft-removes-119-edge-extensions.html
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-06-30
tags:
  - attack.t1071.001
  - attack.command_and_control
level: medium
logsource:
  category: network_connection
  product: windows
detection:
  selection_browser:
    Image|endswith:
      - '\msedge.exe'
      - '\chrome.exe'
      - '\firefox.exe'
    Initiated: true
  filter_known_good:
    DestinationHostname|endswith:
      - .microsoft.com
      - .google.com
      - .mozilla.org
      - .live.com
      - .office.com
      - .windowsupdate.com
      - .msn.com
      - .bing.com
  condition: selection_browser and not filter_known_good
indicators.description: Detects network traffic from browser processes to unknown or suspicious domains, which could indicate a malicious extension like those in the StegoAd campaign communicating with a C2 server.

indicators.name: StegoAd-like Suspicious File Creation in Edge Extension Directory
indicators.pattern:
title: StegoAd-like Suspicious File Creation in Edge Extension Directory
id: a3b4c5d6-e7f8-4a9b-8cde-f1234567890a
status: experimental
description: Detects the creation of image or font files in Microsoft Edge extension directories by non-browser processes, which could indicate steganographic payload delivery as seen in the StegoAd campaign.
references:
  - https://thehackernews.com/2026/06/microsoft-removes-119-edge-extensions.html
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-06-30
tags:
  - attack.t1564.001
  - attack.t1189
  - attack.defense_evasion
level: medium
logsource:
  category: file_event
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    TargetFilename|contains: '\Microsoft\Edge\User Data\Default\Extensions\'
    TargetFilename|endswith:
      - '.png'
      - '.jpg'
      - '.woff'
  filter_browser:
    Image|endswith: '\msedge.exe'
  condition: selection and not filter_browser
indicators.description: Detects the creation of potentially suspicious files (PNG, JPG, WOFF) within browser extension directories, a technique used by StegoAd to hide malicious payloads.

relationships.source_name: StegoAd-like Anomalous Outbound Connections from Browser
StegoAd-like Suspicious File Creation in Edge Extension Directory
